coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free hydrogen peroxide sensor based on Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yancai
Zhang, Yayun
Zhong, Yanmei
Li, Shunxing
coredata.dc:description: The well-designed Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites were synthesized via a facile method, and were used to fabricate an enzyme-free amperometric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor. The size, shape, elementary composition and structure of the nanocomposites were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The outermost layer of the nanocomposites was amorphous carbon, the second layer was Ag and the core was Au. The Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites exhibit attractive activity for electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 according to the electrochemical experiments. It also demonstrates the H2O2 sensor possess well performance with a wide linear range of 5.0μM to 4.75mM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.14μM (S/N =3). Furthermore, the interference from the common interfering species, such as glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid can be effectively avoided. In a word, the Au@Ag@C nanocomposites are promising candidates for enzyme-free H2O2 sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites
Electrocatalytical reduction
Hydrogen peroxide
Enzyme-free sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C
Preparation of the Au@Ag@C/GCE, Au@C/GCE and CDs/GCE
Characterization of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis process of the Au@Ag@C nanocomposites.
TEM image of CDs (a), UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs) and fluorescence spectra (Ex, Em) of CDs (b).
TEM images of the Au@C (a) and the Au@Ag@C (The inset was HRTEM image of edges) (c); EDS of the Au@C (b), the marked region of D and E in Fig. 2c (d,e).
XRD patterns of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of bare GCE (a), CDs/GCE (b), Au@C/GCE (c) and Au@Ag@C/GCE (d) in 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1M KCl, applied ac frequency range: 0.1Hz to 1000kHz.
CVs of bare GCE (a), CDs/GCE (b), Au@C/GCE, (c) and Au@Ag@C/GCE (d) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS (a) and 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM H2O2 (b), scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.
CVs of Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 0mM (a) 1mM, (b) 3mM, (c) 5mM, (d) 7mM, (e) 9mM (f) and 11mM (g) H2O2. Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.

(a) Amperometric response of the bare GCE (a) CDs/GCE, (b) Au@C/GCE (c) and Au@Ag@C/GCE, (d) to the successive additions of different concentrations of H2O2 (5, 10 and 50μM; 0.1 and 0.5mM) in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS at -0.55V; (b) a large scale of amperometric response of the Au@Ag@C/GCE to several μM H2O2; (c) response currents of the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 8a, curve d) to the H2O2 concentration; (d) calibration curve of the current response to the concentration of H2O2 from 5.0 to 75μM of Fig. 8a, curve d; (e) calibration curve of the current response to the concentration of H2O2 from 75μM to 6.575mM of Fig. 8a, curve d.
Amperometric response of Au@Ag@C/GCE at -0.55V with successive additions of 10uL 25mM H2O2, 500mM glucose, 500mM dopamine, 500mM uric acid, 500mM ascorbic acid, 25mM H2O2 to 5mL 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS.
Comparison of the present Au@Ag@C/GCE with other H2O2 sensor.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free hydrogen peroxide sensor based on Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry & Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, PR China. Tel.: +86 596 2591445; fax: +86 596 2520035.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The well-designed Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites were synthesized via a facile method, and were used to fabricate an enzyme-free amperometric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor. The size, shape, elementary composition and structure of the nanocomposites were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The outermost layer of the nanocomposites was amorphous carbon, the second layer was Ag and the core was Au. The Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites exhibit attractive activity for electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 according to the electrochemical experiments. It also demonstrates the H2O2 sensor possess well performance with a wide linear range of 5.0μM to 4.75mM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.14μM (S/N =3). Furthermore, the interference from the common interfering species, such as glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid can be effectively avoided. In a word, the Au@Ag@C nanocomposites are promising candidates for enzyme-free H2O2 sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a kind of excellent antioxidant and one of the products in a variety of biochemical reactions catalyzed by oxidases, such as glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase, choline catalyzed by cholinesterase, and lactic acid catalyzed by lactalase. H2O2 is also a major reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in various diseases, such as cancer [1], cardiovascular disorders [2], and Alzheimer's disease [3]. Therefore, it is very important to detect H2O2 rapidly and accurately in the fields of clinic, pharmaceutical, food security and environmental protection [4–6]. The measurement methods for H2O2 have been explored by various researchers, including resonance light scattering [7], cell imaging [8], electrochemical methods [9], and so on. Compared with other methods, considerable attention has been paid on the electrochemical approach due to its practical advantages which include high sensitivity and selectivity, rapid response, low cost, operation simplicity, easy miniaturization, etc. In the early stage, according to the specificity response of enzyme to the substrate, various nanomaterials were used to immobilize enzyme in order to construct highly sensitive H2O2 sensors [10]. However, it was ineluctable that the catalytic effect of enzyme just preserved in some appropriate conditions. Therefore, it is very important to develop a simple enzyme-free H2O2 sensor.

Bimetallic core−shell nanoparticles (NPs) have attracted growing attention owing to their unique properties, which are different from those of monometallic counterparts and alloys [11]. The well physicochemical properties of synergy between two metals could be coordinated by shape, size and composition [12]. Meanwhile, the bimetallic metal nanoparticles can also exhibit favorable electrocatalytic activity, which takes place on the shell of the NPs and the core metal can markedly affect the performance of the whole NPs. Thus, many bimetallic metal nanoparticles, such as core−shell AuAg [13], AgPt alloy [14] and so on, were used to prepare enzyme-free H2O2 sensor. Au and Ag are ideal choices of the basal material to construct bimetallic metal nanoparticles due to their unique electronic and catalytic properties as well as their good stability, convenience of electron transfer and biocompatibility [15,16], and can be used to develop H2O2 sensor. The synthesized Au−Ag core−shell nanocomposites are generally covered with a layer of carbon nanomaterials, which could protect Au and Ag nanoparticles from oxidation and aggregation. Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, is famous for its water solubility, superior fluorescent, low toxicity, high chemical stability, simple synthesis, remarkable conductivity and biocompatibility [17]. CDs have promising application in the field of bioimaging, pollutant detection and photocatalysis [18].

In this work, we first chose CDs as reductant and raw materials to form carbon film around Au core and obtained Au@C core−shell nanoparticles. The carbon membrane which covered to the Au core was not a dense layer. Usually, the surface of gold nanoparticles presents negative charges. Therefore, the Ag+ may be adsorbed to the surface of Au nanoparticles across the non-dense carbon membrane. After joining with the AA reductant, Ag ions react with AA on the surface of Au particles and become Ag layer between Au and carbon layer. Thus, the Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites were obtained. The nanocomposites were used to fabricate Au@Ag@C-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The Au@Ag@C/GCE showed well electrocatalytical activity toward the reduction of H2O2, and can be used as a nice H2O2 sensor. Furthermore, Au@Ag@C/GCE also exhibits excellent selectivity and stability.

Sodium hydroxide, silver nitrate, hydrogen peroxide (30wt%), oil acid, polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200), sodium dihydrogen phosphate and 12 hydrated sodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Glucose (GL), ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from Instrument Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Chloroauric acid, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical reagents and used without further purification. Redistilled deionized water was used throughout all experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was carried through UV–vis spectrometer (UV-1800PC, Mapada Ltd, Shanghai, China). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy-dispersed spectrum (EDS) were observed with JEM-2100 (JEOL, Ltd., Japan) at 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) test was completed using Bruker D8 Advance (Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) emission was performed using Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorometer (America). Electrochemical experiments were carried out using CHI 650D electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). A conventional three-electrode system was used for all electrochemical experiments, in which a platinum wire used as auxiliary electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) used as reference electrode, and a bare GCE or modified GCE used as working electrode. Additionally, the initial potential of all CV experimental was 0.8V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0mL glucose solution (0.67g/mL) and 10.0mL PEG-200 were mixed to form a pellucid solution. Then the mixture was put into a 540W microwave oven, after 2min, a yellow CDs solution was obtained [19]. Finally, the CDs solution were dialyzed for 3 days with the dialysis membranes of 500 molecular entrapment quantities and diluted to 50.0mL with water, then stored at 4°C for use. The synthesized CDs have carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [20].

Au@C nanoparticles were prepared according to the reference [21] with a little modification, and the details are described as follows: 100μL 1wt% HAuCl4 was added to 3mL CDs aqueous solution. The mixed solution was kept at 120°C silicone oil for 30min to yield a stable red-orange suspension of Au@C nanoparticles.

The procedure for synthesis the Au@Ag@C core-double shell nanocomposites as follows: 4mL 1wt% PVP aqueous solution, 0.1mL Au@C suspension and 1.0mL AgNO3 were mixed. Kept the mixed solution under vigorous stirring for 1min and then added 0.1mL 5wt% AA solution. It can be observed that the mixed suspension turn into shallow orange. Then the suspension was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 15min. The precipitates were collected and washed with redistilled deionized water several times and diluted to 1.0mg/mL. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of the process of synthesis the Au@Ag@C nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Au@Ag@C/GCE, Au@C/GCE and CDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the GCE was polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm α-alumina slurry sequentially to mirror-like and washed successively with distilled water, ethanol and distilled water in ultrasonic bath. The as-prepared Au@Ag@C was dispersed in water with the aid of ultrasonic agitation. Then 10μL Au@Ag@C suspensions were dropped to the GCE surface, and the solvent evaporated at room temperature. The obtained electrode represented as Au@Ag@C/GCE. For comparison, the Au@C/GCE, CDs/GCE was also prepared as the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a is the TEM image of the prepared CDs. Most of the particles are amorphous and the diameter is 10±2nm. The optical properties of metal nanoparticles are affected by the nanoparticle size, shape and dielectric environment [22]. Fig. 1b shows typical UV−vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDs, a strong UV−vis absorption band appeared at 277nm and the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths appeared at 360 and 455nm, respectively, on behalf of the CDs launching yellow-green fluorescence. According to the literature [19], CDs synthesized using PEG as raw material usually contain hydroxyl, which is electron-donating group and can enhance fluorescence. These results were consistent with previous reports [23].

The morphology and the chemical composition of the Au@C and Au@Ag@C were characterized by TEM and EDS. Fig. 2a shows the typical TEM image of Au@C, which the shape is nearly spherical and the diameter is about 24nm. It also clearly observed a low-contrast ring associated with a continuous carbon layer wrap around a high-contrast Au core. This carbon layer wrapping may be formed when CDs were used as reductant, and the carbon layer formed a shell with the thickness of about 1.73nm. It reveals the formation of a core–shell structure of Au@C. Fig. 2 b shows the EDS of one Au@C nanoparticle, which indicates the whole nanoparticle constitute both Au and C element, and also proved the formation of core(Au)−shell(C) structure.

Fig. 2c shows the TEM image of Au@Ag@C, which exhibited a spherical nanoparticle with diameter of about 43nm. Fig. 2d and e, respectively, exhibit the EDS of the regions D and E on the spherical nanoparticle in Fig. 2c. From Fig. 2d, the weight percentage of C, Ag and Au were 16.74, 17.13 and 66.13%, respectively, and the weight percentage of C, Ag and Au were 0.62, 1.33 and 98.05%, respectively, from Fig. 2e. That is to say, the content of silver at edges (region D) of the nanoparticle was more than the region near center (region E). As shown from the HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 2c, the outermost layer was the amorphous carbon. In combination with the EDS analysis of each region, we can conclude that the second layer was Ag and the core was Au. The thickness of C and Ag layer was 1.67 and 2.3nm, respectively. According to the above TEM and EDS results, we can conclude the formation of core-double shell Au@Ag@C nanocomposites.

Fig. 3 displays the XRD patterns of CDs, Au@C and Au@Ag@C. The XRD pattern of the CDs shows a broad peak at around 20°, which indicates the presence of a non-crystalline material and amorphous nature. The same XRD peak position (around 20°) have been observed for CDs in literatures [24,25]. As to the Au@C, we can observe four diffraction peaks at 38.34, 44.52, 64.88 and 77.8° separately, which correspond to (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes of Au [26]. The XRD results of the Au@Ag@C was same as the Au@C due to the same structure (face-centered cubic, fcc) and lattice constant of Au and Ag monometallic phase. The XRD results further demonstrate the formation of core-double shell Au@Ag@C nanocomposites.

EIS can exhibit the impedance changes of different modified electrodes during the modification process. The EIS curve consists of two parts, one is the semicircular part on behalf of higher frequencies and the diameter is equal to the electron transfer resistance (R et), which corresponds to the electron transfer limited process. The other is the linear part at lower frequencies which corresponds to the diffusion process at the electrode and electrolyte interface. Fig. 4 shows the EIS of different electrodes in 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1M KCl. As shown in Fig. 4, the EIS of the bare GCE displayed quite small semicircle diameters, while the R et of CDs/GCE was estimated to 7500Ω. To the Au@C/GCE, the R et was decreased to 3200Ω (Fig. 4, curve c). This can be attributed to the Au@C nanoparticles, which have more electron transfer passages. The R et of the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 4, curve d) was estimated to be 2700Ω, which was smaller than the Au@C/GCE. The smaller R et value indicates well conductivity of the Au@Ag@C/GCE, which can be ascribed to the Ag shell facilitating the electron transportation as a bridge between the Au core and the C shell. Therefore, the Au@Ag@C could depress the resistance of the sensing platform and accelerate electron transfer rate. This also proved that the Au@Ag@C nanocomposites could act as an excellent electronic substrate and its core-double shell structure could contribute to much larger response surface and electron transfer passages.

Fig. 5 a shows the CVs of bare GCE, CDs/GCE, Au@C/GCE and Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS without H2O2. The Au@C/GCE (Fig. 5a, curve c) shows no obvious redox peaks when the potential is higher than 0V. As expected, the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 5a, curve d) shows the redox peaks, respectively, at the potential higher than 0V that may cause by Ag which can originate from its easy oxidation nature [13]. To bare GCE, no redox peaks is observed within the applied potential (Fig. 5a, curve a). However, there is a reductive peak at around −0.5V to CDs/GCE (Fig. 5a, curve b). For Au@Ag@C/GCE and Au@C/GCE, the peak current increased slightly at around −0.5V. It is caused by the reduction of the oxide group on CDs and carbon film [19,27,28]. In addition, the current of the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 5a, curve d) was higher than that the other modified GCEs at −0.5V. This may arise from the Ag between C and Au, which enhanced the conductivity of the film and further increased the reductive peak current of C film.

Fig. 5b shows the CVs of bare GCE, CDs/GCE, Au@C/GCE and Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS with 1mM H2O2. It can be seen that the CV displayed a sensitive and high reductive peak at around −0.52V of the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 5b, curve d), while the CDs/GCE and Au@C/GCE displayed lower reductive peak at around −0.52V (curve b and c of Fig. 5b) and the bare GCE didn’t display any reductive peak at same potential (curve a of Fig. 5b). The CDs/GCE and Au@C/GCE displayed weak electrocatalytic effect which caused by the CDs and C film (curve b and c of Fig. 5b), their mechanism can be represented as [29]:
(1) C+H2O2 +e→OH(ads) +[C-OH]−
(2) C+OH(ads) +e→[C-OH]−
(3) 2[C-OH]− +2H+ →2H2O+2C

What's more, the maximum reduction peak current at around −0.52V of the Au@Ag@C/GCE also should be attributed to the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2. The good electrocatalytic activity of the Au@Ag@C/GCE should be benefited by the synergy properties of carbon film and the conductive Ag. The catalytic mechanism of Ag for the reduction of H2O2 is proposed as follows [30,31]:
(4) 2Au@Ag(0)@C+H2O2 →2Au@Ag(1)@C-OH
(5) 2Au@Ag(1)@C-OH+2e+2H+ →2Au@Ag(0)@C+2H2O

Combined with Fig. 5a and b, we can conclude that the Au@Ag@C/GCE possess well electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 reduction. The good electrocatalytic activity on the Au@Ag@C/GCE should be concerned with the synergy properties of three elements (C, Ag, Au), core-double shell structure [32] and nanometer size effect [33].

Fig. 6 shows CVs of the Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing different concentrations of H2O2. The reduction peak current (at about −0.52V) increases clearly with increasing of H2O2 concentration. It is also obviously observed that the reduction peak potential shifts from −0.44 to −0.57V and the reduction peak becomes broader. It is a typical electrocatalytic reduction process of H2O2 according to the literature [34].

The effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 was also discussed. Fig. S1a displays the CVs of the Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS containing 1mM H2O2 at different scan rates. The redox peak currents increased gradually with increasing the scan rates. However, the redox peak potential almost remained unchanged. As shown in the Fig. S1b, the reduction peak current of H2O2 was linearly with the scan rate. The regression equation was I pc(μA)=0.14(±0.002) v (mV/s)+14.46 (±0.53) (n =13, r =0.9989). It indicated that the electrode reaction corresponds to a surface-controlled process.

Fig. S2a shows the current-time curves of the Au@Ag@C/GCE in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS with successive additions of 10μL 0.25M H2O2 at different applied potential (−0.45, −0.50, −0.55, −0.60 and −0.65V). From Fig. S2a, the response current of H2O2 increased with the applied potential decreasing from −0.45 to −0.60V, and the response current decreased at −0.65V. That is to say, the electrocatalytic response of H2O2 reached to maximize at −0.60V. Fig. S2b shows the ratio of response current to background current, namely the ratio of signal to noise (S/N), which increased first and then decreased, and the maximum ratio occurred at −0.55V. Thus, an applied potential of −0.55V was chosen as the working potential for further amperometric investigation.

Fig. 7 a shows the typical amperometric current-time curves of the bare GCE, CDs/GCE, Au@C/GCE and Au@Ag@C/GCE to the successive additions of different concentrations of H2O2 to 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS at −0.55V. With the additions of H2O2, small amperometric response was observed at the bare GCE, CDs/GCE and Au@C/GCE (Fig. 7a, curves a–c). However, the largest, well-defined, amperometric response can be observed at the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 7a, curve d) with successive additions of 5μM to 0.5mM H2O2. From the magnified current-time curves of Au@Ag@C/GCE in Fig. 8 b, it can be clearly observed that it could achieve the steady-state current within 5s. This indicated that Au@Ag@C/GCE have an extraordinarily rapid and sensitively response to H2O2.

Besides, obvious current response could be observed with the additions of H2O2 from several micrometers to several millimeters for the Au@Ag@C/GCE (Fig. 7c). It demonstrated two linear ranges for H2O2, which one is from 5.0 to 75μM with sensitivity as high as 76.02μAmM−1 cm−2 (Fig. 7d), another is from 75 to 4.75mM with sensitivity of 19.12μAmM−1 cm−2 (Fig. 7e). The calibration curves in Figs. 7d and 8e are I(μA)=5.37 (±0.04) [H2O2] (mM)+0.34 (±0.002) (r =0.9998, n =10) and I(μA)=1.35 (±0.01) [H2O2] (mM)+0.84 (±0.02) (r =0.9992, n =26), respectively. When signal to noise was 3, the detection limit was 0.14 and 8.32μM, respectively.

From the above experimental results, there was a higher sensitivity at lower H2O2 concentration. The reason may be the H2O2 molecules freely diffused with being ignored interaction between molecules, leading to high sensitivity. In contrast, as reported in the literature [35], the H2O2 molecular interaction led to lower sensitivity at high concentrations. Besides it can be seen from Table 1 , the sensitivity of Au@Ag@C-modified electrode is obviously higher than most of other nonenzyme H2O2 sensor. The large proportion of surface active sites and the better electrocatalytical activity of these bimetallic core-double shell nanoparticles might also contribute to it.

The anti-interference effect of the sensor toward glucose (GL), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA) was also investigated. GL, DA, UA and AA are the most commonly existing interference species in the physiological environment. Fig. 8 displays the amperometric response of the sensor with the additions of 10μL, 25mM H2O2, 500mM GL, 500mM DA, 500mM UA, 500mM AA and 25mM H2O2, orderly. The concentration of H2O2 was 20 times less than interferences, but the current response of H2O2 was far higher than interferences. The experimental results indicate that these substances have no obvious interference to the H2O2 determination, which implies Au@Ag@C/GCE has a good selectivity to the determination of H2O2.

When the Au@Ag@C/GCE was stored in 0.1M pH 7.0 PBS for 30 days, there was no obvious current change in response to 1.0mM H2O2. In the same concentration, we continuously determinate six times, the relative standard deviation (R.S.D) is 3.8%. It indicates Au@Ag@C/GCE has good stability and reproducibility.

We synthesized core-double shell structure Au@Ag@C nanocomposites via a facile approach and used to construct H2O2 sensor. The nanocomposites have good electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 reduction. Morever, the modified electrode performs a wonderful sensitivity, selectivity, low detection limit, fast response, good stability and anti-interference ability toward the determination of H2O2. These experimental results demonstrate that Au@Ag@C nanocomposites are an attractive material for the fabrication of efficient hydrogen peroxide sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175115), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province in China (2012J05031 and 2012Y0065), and the Innovation Base Foundation for Graduate Students Education of Fujian Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2015.04.102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Regulation of autophagy by reactive oxygen species (ROS): implications for cancer progression and treatment
Oxidative stress and cardiovascular injury, part I: basic mechanisms and in vivo monitoring of ROS
Reactive oxygen species: role in the development of cancer and various chronic conditions
A simple and innovative route to prepare a novel carbon nanotube/prussian blue electrode and its utilization as a highly sensitive H2O2 amperometric sensor
Hydrogen peroxide vapour decontamination of surfaces artificially contaminated with norovirus surrogate feline calicivirus
Doxorubicin induces apoptosis in normal and tumor cells via distinctly different mechanisms intermediacy of H2O2 and p53-dependent pathways
A simple and sensitive resonance scattering spectral method for determination of hydroxyl radical in Fenton system using rhodamine S and its application to screening the antioxidant
Fluorescent probes for the detection of hydrogen peroxide in biological systems
A non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor based on poly (vinyl alcohol)–multiwalled carbon nanotubes–platinum nanoparticles hybrids modified glassy carbon electrode
An amperometric hydrogen peroxide biosensor based on Co3O4 nanoparticles and multiwalled carbon nanotube modified glassy carbon electrode
Structural analysis of polymer-protected palladium/platinum bimetallic clusters as dispersed catalysts by using extended X-ray absorption fine structure spectroscopy
Nanoalloys: from theory to applications of alloy clusters and nanoparticles
Core–shell Au/Ag nanoparticles embedded in silicate sol–gel network for sensor application towards hydrogen peroxide
Design of AgM bimetallic alloy nanostructures (M=Au, Pd, Pt) with tunable morphology and peroxidase-like activity
Modified titanium surface with gelatin nano gold composite increases osteoblast cell biocompatibility
Carbon quantum dots-doped CdS microspheres with enhanced photocatalytic performance
Carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability under visible light
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Synthesis of gold@carbon dots composite nanoparticles for surface enhanced Raman scattering
The optical properties of metal nanoparticles: the influence of size, shape, and dielectric environment
Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off–on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Au/graphene hydrogel: synthesis, characterization and its use for catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots, TrAC
A novel nonenzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor based on multi-wall carbon nanotube/silver nanoparticle nanohybrids modified gold electrode
Low-potential amperometric determination of hydrogen peroxide with a carbon paste electrode modified with nanostructured cryptomelane-type manganese oxides
